turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: We'll hear argument now in number oh one six seventy-nine, Gonzaga University and Rob- Roberta S League versus John Doe. Mr. Roberts. [Inaudible]

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Chief Justice, may it please the Court: In nineteen seventy-four, when it en- enacted the Family Educational r- Rights and Privacy Act, Congress conditioned Federal funding for educational institutions on the institution not having a policy or practice of releasing student records without consent. Congress did not phrase this condition in terms of individual rights. It did not, for example, follow the model of title nine, enacted two years earlier and also dealing with educational institutions, and say something like, no student at a school receiving Federal funds shall have his records released without his consent. Instead, Congress proceeded more indirectly. It said that no funds shall be made available to any institution having a policy or practice of releasing student records without consent. The statute is directed to the Secretary of Education. He's the one who makes Federal funds available, not to the institution receiving the funds, and certainly not to the individual student. This Court's cases establish that that is a distinction that makes a difference. In Cannon, for example, the Court said there would be...

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: far less reason -- Mr. Roberts, isn't it...

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: primarily a distinction that makes a difference in c- connection with whether there's an implied cause of action, rather than whether nineteen eighty-three authorized a cause of action?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: In the implied right of action question there are two questions, did Congress intend to create a right, and did Congress intend to provide a judicial remedy? In the nineteen eighty-three context, there are two questions, did Congress intend to create a right, and did Congress intend to preclude resort to the nineteen eighty-three remedy, so that first question I think is the same under both categories of cases, and as the Court said in Cannon, if Congress phrases the statute as quo- quote, as a prohibition on the disbursement of public funds, there's far less reason to think that they intended a private remedy.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: This- The the statute does talk about rights of students and rights rights of parents.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, of course, the word rights does not appear in the disclosure provision, subsection B, and in Pennhurst, where the Court was dealing with the developmentally disabled bill of rights, the Court explained that just because the statute uses the word rights doesn't mean that it creates a nineteen eighty-three right.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Yes, I recognize in the one section that we're talking about here you have a stronger argument than the other, but if we a- assume for the moment would have a nineteen eighty-three cause of action under the whole act without going down provision by provision, then I I do think you have to recognize that w- the act does talk about rights of students, rights of parents t- to look at files, et cetera.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, first of all, the Court in Blessing said that you don't look at the whole act.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: eighty-two.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: I think it's that latter rationale that might be stronger for your case.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, whatever rights, whether you're talking e- putting aside the question whether it's a nineteen eighty-three right or a right to participate in the process that's established under the statute, it is part of the policy or practice that the Secretary of Education is to look to in deciding whether to disburse funds.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: deal -- twelve...

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: twelve A of our statutory appendix, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: It says to the Secretary, you set up an office to investigate, process, review, and adjudicate complaints about violations under FERPA.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: You you say violations of this section.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: There's no violation unless there's a policy.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: to investigate any individual complaint, un- unless the person comes in and says, not only do they do it to me, but this is their policy, right?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: It's as evidence that there might be a problem with the school's policy, and this is what makes it different, for example, from the Wright case.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: I guess that that's all that the plaintiff could a- could accomplish in court anyway.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: That's correct.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: So the Secretary's enforcement authority is coextensive with what the court would be able to do.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I- In terms of the scope of liabil-

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: You'd need an allegation of a policy or practice.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Exactly, but it is the fact that Congress focused on the policy or practice that helps establish that they were not concerned with individual instances of disclosure.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: Well, Mr. Roberts, why are they mutually exclusive? I mean, the the Secretary has this authority, and I think your argument would be more impressive if this were a large operation.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: It's it's very important to keep in mind the distinction behow- between how matters are handled before the Family Policy Compliance Office and in court.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Do we know -- do we know...

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: from the maybe the attorn- Solicitor General can tell us if the seven people are overworked?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: In fact, in practice most of what they do is field questions from the school, how do we handle this situation, what do we do...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: about this?

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: order? I mean tha- one of the points that was made is that if e- records are about to be divulged, say, to a newspaper, and the student or the parent wants an s- immediate stop order, you can go into court and get a TRO.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, there certainly is.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: This office can't really give relief to any individual, however, right, except to tell the tell the school not to release wrongfully release records in the future, right?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: The focus of the office is in vindicating what the statute provides.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: So all -- all you have to...

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: do is eliminate the policy, and everything that's happened in the past is water over the dam and...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Because the --

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: go and sin no more is what the Secretary says, right?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Because the statute is directed to prospective compliance, not retrospective compensation for injuries.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: The nineteen eighty-th- --

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: What do you do about the the language where it says, no funds shall be made available to a school that effectively prevents, et cetera, th- the students th- ri- giv- it says, of the right to inspect.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: The direct response is that you've left out words in the quote, which is that no funds shall be made available to an institution that has a policy or practice, and the question is, is Congress focusing on protecting individual rights in, as the Court said in Blessing, an individual way, or are they addressing a systemic concern.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Subsection B does not talk about rights.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: And finally, I'll --  th- the answer  --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: b- be sure I've understand your answer.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: I think not,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: because --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: The the right to inspect is not an independent and freestanding right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Subsection B --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: initial submission is that this is not a rights-creating statute, it just er- addre- but but I don't know where the right comes from that they refer to in that section and also in...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: -- in on twelve A informing...

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: parents and students of rights under this section.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Under this section.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: repeatedly in --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: that the stu- they they would have had the rights described herein even if this statute had not been passed?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: No.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: action.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: opportunity in the statute wherever the statute used the word rights.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well, the statute doesn't use the words right under subsection B.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: right.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: twelve three two G B is not the se- section at issue here.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: No...

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: What section is is the one at issue?

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: twelve thirty-two G B is the section at issue.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, but B...

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: does say, th- the parents of students the right to inspect and review.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: That's A, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: B is on six A,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I gather is what you're --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: B is on six A and it does not refer to rights.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Ms. Millett, we'll hear from you.

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: If I could begin by responding to Justice Kennedy's question about whether the Family Policy Compliance Office is overworked, I will tell you that they do work very hard, but they do they handle for a small staff, they handle an amazing amount of work, and have been doing so for twenty-eight years under this statute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: as they go through the investigation stages.

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: Hmm?

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: That's three letters a day.

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: Yes, but they also I'm I'm not done with my list if you'll forgive me.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: That's ten phone calls a day.

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: That's right and well and well over a well over a thousand e-mails, and I think one of the reasons that there isn't I mean, if you look at the the legal landscape out there, too, there hasn't been an e- enormous volume of nineteen eighty-three actions, and that is because the Secretary has been very successful, I think, in communicating and enforcing this in an informal manner with the universities.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: action...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: about the idea that this could be bifurcated, that it that it orders a right to inspect, because that isn't going to be too tough, you let the person look at the records, but there's a policy of disclosure.

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: If I could ask answer that in two stages.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: first three factors listed in Blessing, Congress intended that it benefit the plaintiff plaintiff, and it not be beyond judicial competence...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Not not be --

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: must be a unam- unambiguously binding obligation on the State, it would s- seem to me that those are met here, and that you then have to go to the next part of the test which is th- th- th- that that creates a s- presumption that there is a right.

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: Hm.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: fails, a statute that --

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: does seem to me that it talks of the student and the parent...

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: Mhm.

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: in very specific terms, and it uses uses the term right.

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: Well, again, this this Court made clear in Pennhurst that you can't just look at the word right in isolation.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: precise language, but it contains --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: same question I asked Mr. Roberts, I suppose, but the first sentence of twelve thirty-two G A one A refers to a policy of denying access, denying the right to inspect and review education record. [:records] In your view, is that right a federally created right, or is that a right created by some other source and, if so, what is the source of the right?

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: I am sorry, I'm having there were...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: too many numbers in that. it's like G one --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: of the blue brief, and the first, very first sentence in the in the statute ends by saying the right to inspect and review the education...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: records of their children,

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: and my question is whether you think that is a federally created right and, if not, what is the source of that right?

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: I think it's it's not for- I've two answers.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: pre-existing moral or legal -- Mhm.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: Do you think the right to which the statute refers is a federally created right,

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: Mhm.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: or right with a different source?

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: I think what the st- the statute is creating there is a Federal overlay to protect pre- as as as was said in the legislative history on page seventeen...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: of our brief,

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: So that if a school came back and said, in our State there's no such right,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: then the statute would not apply?

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: Congress felt that when they said preexisting moral or legal rights, that ther- there was a sense of Congress that this is a type of, n- not not in a right sense that we use in for purposes of nineteen eighty-three actions, but con- the legislative history was that Congress had a sense here that this was something all individuals should have.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: But -- this...

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: i- It It is som- something they should have by reason of this statute, and therefore it's rightly created, or by reason of some preexisting rule of law in some at some other source?

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: Well, i- Congress' language was preexisting moral or legal, rights and so I'm I'm not sure what one considers...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: If it's another source, it seems to me that...

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: the State or the institution could say, in this locality there is no such right, and that would make the statute totally inapplicable.

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: No, no it wouldn't, because what you still have is, once you take these funds you have a Federal overlay.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: doesn't matter, which you have --

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: nonexistent right, if I understand you...

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: Well, then thed- there's a fed there is there's no doubt that there's a Federal level of protection here for privacy, and it wa- it it's at a aggregated, collective system-wide level.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: level of   --

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: Mr. Roberts used the word obligation, you stay away from the the term, right, in the statute.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: or would you say it's a right...

turns.name: JUSTICE KENNEDY
turns.text: that can be enforced through a comprehensive administrative scheme?

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: That that's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: that in fact --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: I think when he used the word, opportunity, that this is kind of the that the scheme of this section is sort of an if-then sort of scheme.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Yes. yeah th- our ar-

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: there's there's there's an opportunity.

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: Th- th- That's right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Thank you, Ms. Millett.

turns.name: PATRICIA A. MILLETT
turns.text: Thank you.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Ms. Brinkmann, we'll hear from you.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Mr. Chief Justice, and may it please the Court: In FERPA, Congress gave parents the right to prevent the release of certain education records.

turns.text: Now, oi- you're reading from nine A, Ms. Brinkmann?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Whereabouts on page nine A?

turns.text: Your Honor, paragraph two begins that no...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: fund shall be given to an agency,

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: and explains that has a policy or...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I don't see it either. I'm looking at page --

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: nine A of the red brief.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: It's on page eight A of the bu-

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: You're switching briefs on us.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: I'm sorry.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: It's on page eight A of the blue brief, if you prefer that.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Yes, it does.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Unless.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Unless.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Now, if you don't have a policy or practice, the whole provision doesn't apply.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: th- If you don't have a policy or practice of releasing information other than under the preceding section B one, you're correct,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Your Honor. And B two A is --

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: B one says you can't a school can't have a policy of releasing without consent, other than to certain...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: categories.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: you read the word policy, what policy? I think Justice Scalia is reading it as, what policy?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: It's a policy --

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: releasing records without written consent.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: That's not what not na- that's not what B two A says.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Hm.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: any personally identifiable information, other than directory information, or is permitted under paragraph one.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: I'm I'm really not following you.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Unless.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Okay.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: So...

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: But but that whole provision, no funds shall be made available, only applies to an educational agency or institution which has a policy or practice of releasing.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Absolutely.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: policy or practice of releasing, it's entirely exempt from that provision.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: That's correct, Your Honor, because they did not Congress did not intend to go after inadvertent releases.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: twoA.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: They may choose not to.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: parents.

turns.name: JUSTICE O'CONNOR
turns.text: be a scheme, at least as I read it, just directed at when Federal funds are going to be given to a school, and you determine that by whether the school has a particular policy or practice, and and the remedy is withholding funds.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Your Honor, in addition to the language, unless there is, our our position is, because there is that requirement, unless there is written consent from the parent, Congress intended to directly benefit the parents and to say to the parents in a particular situation, you can say no, I don't want this information released.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: benefit the child.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: because i- I mean, if the information is released and the parent says, I object, the institution can say, oh, I'm sorry, that was just just just a mistake.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: There is, Your Honor, because the if they do have a policy in process, it's akin to the standard that the Court adopted in Gebser, and here Congress did that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: parents, and again I have to --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: if if they don't get consent from the parents, the express consequence is no funds shall be made available.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Court, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Well, Your Honor, there's never been a formula.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: by these conditions.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: stated you my question precisely.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: No statute of that language has ever come before the Court, Your Honor, and all I'm saying is, there are many other s- cases in which statutes have been found to accord rights under section nineteen eighty-three that don't have that no-student-shall language.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: What about yu- you say, you agree there is no example of a ca- we've decided where the term is no funds shall?

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN

turns.text: wherts- [:where's ] where the statute's i- th- the footnote you're quoting r- s- speaks in terms of, or addresses the no funds shall?

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: The precise language in that statute which is quoted in that footnote says that no Federal funds payments shall be made, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Two more. You have...

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Yes.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: So we have right the the word right used in B as well as in A, or at least with reference to B as well as with...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Much more...

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: precisely, your Honor here because they are specifically talking not just about B generically, but about pe- permission or consent.

turns.text: Why does right refer to B? I mean, rights could refer to A.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Because the whole provision of D refers to permission or consent, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: or --

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: permission or consent of and...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Yes. Yes, but --

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: that be the first is this, and the other is that.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: But if you look at the structure of the provision, they are referring to the actual permission or consent, because that's when you would need to know, do I go to the when I for a college student,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: do I go to the --

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: after he's eighteen is a right that goes to the student, not to the parent.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: But Your Honor, this is specifically addressing the permission or consent...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: provision. You can tell by the...

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: heading of subsection D.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: required here. i- It would be in the regulations, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Even if even if we...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: we say that you met the three Blessing standards, Justice O'Connor said in that opinion, there's something more, and that the more is what seems to be the strongest emphasis of the case that Mr. Roberts and Ms. Millett made, and that is that Congress created an enforcement scheme that they meant to be it, that would be incompatible with individual enforcement.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Actually that, ironically, b- b- leads me to my third point, in fact.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Under petitioner's interpretation --

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor, and and I think with...

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: you you advance the ball at all by saying what they were worried about was precisely this thing.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Well, it was the point that Justice Ginsburg brought up before, which actually I think responds to your inquiry.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: But that that's...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: It may not be the ideal remedy.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Your Honor, actually it's much more complicated than that.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: [Inaudible]

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: that nineteen eighty-three really doesn't fit this pattern, because why even, why should certain kinds of institutions be stopped, and others not,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: doing the same thing?

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Because the relationship of students with a private school is different than a relationship with a public school.

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: But doesn't the student have the same, whether we're going to call it right or opportunity, in in the private school with respect to records,

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: like a...

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: private university?

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Only if the school receives Federal funds.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Secondary and elementary --

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Only universities, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Ms. Brinkmann, can I can I  come back...

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: to your your assertion that that there that there is no right to an injunction, you can't get an injunction under under this act, but you can't you can't get an injunction, even if we accept your your theory of the act.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Absolutely, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Absolutely, and in -- Because in this...

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: which suggests that you're not vindicating a private right of of yours, that that that that that somehow what Congress is concerned with is the existence of a policy or practice that it doesn't like.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: With all due respect --

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: even though you're being harmed by this release, under your theory you can't get an injunction against it.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I I respectfully disagree.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: a policy or...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: prac-

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: absolutely could get an injunction, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: h- How so?

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Because you needed the testimony of one witness in this case who said, we do this all of the time.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: before --

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: that witness, and if you don't have such a witness, you cannot get an injunction, isn't that right?

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: That's right.

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: Is that...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: can...

turns.name: JUSTICE BREYER
turns.text: I ask you one question on the practicality? if assuming all the language is ambiguous, et cetera, and I'd like you to remove this image from my mind.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: I think I have at least five answers.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: People comply.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: if if the force if we accept the force of your argument, then I think we'd we'd have to say, well, Congress really didn't need to bother with the centralized administration provision, and yet Congress did put it in, and it seems to me the most likely reason that it put it in is the reason that Justice Breyer just gave.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I think --

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: you may have may have mayed have made a good argument for getting rid of it, but as long as it's there, it seems to have the same lesson that his his question suggests.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: I think that the the FPCO office serves a admirable and y- meritorious role.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: But that may be a very good argument for saying that what Congress had in mind, in effect, in in confining the enforcement the way it seems to have done by this exclusive authority provision works in the general run of cases, and therefore there is not a good reason to say that Congress probably would have wanted this private right of action with the punitive damages.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: I think it works generally, and then you look at the Blessing inquiry to see if Congress intended to create a right, they intended to create a right from all of those reasons I have said.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: mandatory.

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: we're at the we're at we're beyond stage one, two...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: three and we're saying, okay,

turns.name: JUSTICE SOUTER
turns.text: are there particular reasons to think that they that they did not.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Then it's presumptively available, a section nineteen eighty-three action.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: in the Sea Clammers case and Smith V Robinson.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: W- why isn't the the theory of ce- centralized administration, spe- spelled out in the statute, with the Secretary's office doing this thing, why doesn't that overcome the presumption?

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Because the presumption has to be overcome by an enforcement scheme, an administrative scheme that supplants the section nineteen eighty-three that has some address for a private...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, certainly it doesn't...

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: have to be a duplicate of section nineteen...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: eighty-three,

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: or there'd be no point to saying it supplants...

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Absolutely, Your Honor, but here there is absolutely no availability for any remedy for an individual injury, and Sea Clammers...

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Miss -- Miss Brink --

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Wait a minute.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Not under B two A, if they have released records.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: case --

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: can you give us one other example of a right that depends upon whether the person allegedly violating the right has done it before?

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Yes, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: policy or practice of doing it? For example, you know, your right to be free from unreasonable searches and seizures.

turns.name: JUSTICE STEVENS
turns.text: I suppose you're going to tell us about the Monell case.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: I was going to cite the Monell case.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: I think that's exactly what the Monell case is about.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: on whom you can sue.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Your Honor, a policy or practice may not have injured anyone in the past.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: Maybe, but it's a very strange right.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: This is the fact that...

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: rights question, too, that I yu- you're relying on the use of the right, of the term, right, in the statute.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: I don't believe so, Your Honor.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: have to --

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: it refer to existing State privacy rights, or just to sort of a moral a moral notion of what things should be kept private?

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Well, I have to emphasize, our statutory argument about rights is not based solely on the twelve thirty-two G D referenced rights.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Well, Ms. Brinkmann, th-

turns.name: JUSTICE GINSBURG
turns.text: there haven't been other cases where substantial monetary damages and punitive damages have been available, and maybe that's the concern.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: It's not the same conduct, Your Honor, if I may.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Thank...

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: you, Ms. Brinkmann.

turns.name: BETH S. BRINKMANN
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Mr. Roberts, you have four minutes remaining.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.

turns.name: JUSTICE SCALIA
turns.text: They're answerable to us, presumably.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE ROBERTS
turns.text: Well It is implausible to suppose that the same Congress that was so worried about multiple interpretations of the law from the regional offices of one Department would have been perfectly content and, in fact, intended to confer the right for every one of sixty-two million students to go into court in a nineteen eighty-three action.

turns.name: CHIEF JUSTICE REHNQUIST
turns.text: Thank you, Mr. Roberts.

turns.name: UNKNOWN
turns.text: Thank you, Your Honor.
